hero.subtitle: Authorization Code + PKCE Exchange for web and mobile — X Developer Portal setup, API reference, and frontend examples.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: X / Twitter login is optional and controlled by TWITTER_AUTH_ENABLED. When disabled, Twitter endpoints return 503 Service Unavailable. JWTs are never placed in the redirect URL; the API issues a one-time exchangeCode (Redis, 60 seconds). The same POST /auth/exchange endpoint is shared with Google and Facebook. X requires PKCE, so the API uses Express session middleware.
items: Same token response shape as POST /auth/login

console.title: X Developer Portal
console.description: Create a Project and App with OAuth 2.0, enable Request email from users, and set the Callback URI to match TWITTER_CALLBACK_URL.
steps: Open developer.x.com → Projects & Apps → create a Project and App
Under User authentication settings, enable OAuth 2.0
Enable “Request email from users” (required for users.email / confirmed_email)
Copy Client ID and Client Secret into your .env
Scopes used by this API: tweet.read, users.read, offline.access, users.email

env.title: Environment variables
env.description: Loaded from .env via src/config/twitter-oauth.config.ts (namespace twitterOAuth on ConfigService). See .env.example. Set SESSION_SECRET (or rely on JWT_SECRET) for PKCE sessions.

roles.title: Default roles for new Twitter users
roles.description: New Twitter users receive roles: [] by default (same as password register). Set TWITTER_OAUTH_DEFAULT_ROLES in .env to a comma-separated list of Role values (e.g. user or user,manager). Existing users linked by email keep their current roles.

flow.title: Sequence
items: User consents on X; browser hits GET /api/v1/auth/twitter/callback (PKCE via session)
API upserts user, stores exchangeCode in Redis, redirects to redirect?code=...
Client POSTs { code } to /api/v1/auth/exchange and receives JWTs

start.title: 1. Start OAuth
start.description: Opens X consent. Query redirect must match a prefix in TWITTER_REDIRECT_ALLOWLIST (or omit to use the first allowlist entry).

callback.title: 2. Callback
callback.description: Handled by the API. Redirects to your app with ?code= (or &code= if the URL already has a query).

exchange.title: 3. Exchange code
exchange.description: Public endpoint shared with Google and Facebook. Consumes the code (single use) and returns the same shape as login.

web.title: Frontend — Web (SPA)
web.description: Typical flow: button starts OAuth in the same window (or popup); a callback route on your frontend origin reads code from the query string and calls exchange.
notes: Add your frontend callback URL to TWITTER_REDIRECT_ALLOWLIST (e.g. http://localhost:5173/auth/callback)
TWITTER_CALLBACK_URL must point at the Nest API, not the SPA
Store accessToken / refreshToken securely (memory + httpOnly cookie patterns as you prefer)

mobile.title: Frontend — Mobile (deep link)
mobile.description: Use a custom scheme (or universal link) in the allowlist. After X, the API redirects to myapp://success?code=.... The app opens, parses the code, and calls exchange.
notes: Register the scheme in the OS / Expo app.json
Exchange over HTTPS against your API base URL

security.title: Security notes
items: Redirect URLs must match TWITTER_REDIRECT_ALLOWLIST prefixes
Exchange codes are single-use and expire in 60 seconds (Redis)
PKCE code_verifier is stored in the Express session cookie
Existing local users are linked by verified Twitter email (twitterId set; roles unchanged)
Twitter-only users have password null — password login / change-password will fail until a password is set
users.email + confirmed_email are required; login fails if X does not return an email

userModel.title: User model
userModel.description: Postgres entity / interface fields used by X / Twitter login:
items: twitterId — unique nullable X / Twitter subject ID
isActive — set true for new Twitter users

samples.fullPageRedirect: // Full-page redirect to Nest → X → back to SPA
samples.persistTokens: // persist tokens as your app requires
